bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Early South-English Legendary; or, Lives of Saints. I. MS. Laud, 108, in the Bodleian Library, Early English Text Society, Original Series 87 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1887).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 87
bibliographic.title: The Early South-English Legendary; or, Lives of Saints. I. MS. Laud, 108, in the Bodleian Library

entities.description: The immediate printed source of the U-M encoding. Horstmann uses Laud Misc. 108 as principal running-text witness, supplies defined lacunae from Vernon, Harley 2277, and Cotton Julius D IX, and prints a substantial variant apparatus.
entities.label: Carl Horstmann, The Early South-English Legendary (1887)
notes: Contemporary EETS matter projected a broader standard collection, additions, glossary, and source study; the title's 'I' does not by itself establish a missing narrative continuation to this exact Laud text.
Bibliographic records state that no further volume of this title was published.

rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1887 and is outside the U.S. copyright term. This conclusion does not automatically cover modern scans or interfaces.
rights.component: 1887 edition text, apparatus, and public-domain editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States

dateStatements.label: containing composite codex
dateStatements.value: Late thirteenth to fourteenth century, with fourteenth-century additions

dateStatements.label: target main hand A
dateStatements.value: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century

dateStatements.label: target appendix hand D
dateStatements.value: Middle or third quarter of the fourteenth century

entities.description: Composite parchment codex. The target is discontiguous: the main hand-A legendary selection occupies ff. 23r–198r and later hand-D Saints Blaise and Cecilia ff. 228v–233v. The target excludes ff. 1–22, intervening ff. 198r–228r, and the material following the shared f. 233v boundary.

holding.notes: Target main ff. 23r–198r and appendix ff. 228v–233v; f. 198r and f. 233v are shared textual boundary surfaces.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108

physicalDescription.extent: ii modern paper + i medieval parchment + 240/243 leaves; modern foliation 1–239 with 170 repeated
physicalDescription.format: Composite codex with target copied principally by hand A and later appendix by hand D
notes: Hand A's scribal dialect is associated with West Oxfordshire; hand D's language with western England, perhaps Gloucestershire, overlaid by East Anglian forms.
Latin and Anglo-Norman rubrics occur in the target. Possible Oxford production is tentative and is not a work-composition claim.

rights.basis: The anonymous medieval texts and their 1887 publication predate modern copyright terms by centuries; this does not cover modern photographs, catalog records, editions, or interfaces.

next_steps: Collate the exact Horstmann transition lines against full source images or authoritative transcriptions.
Preserve the established broad DIMEV loci until exact endpoints are proven.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact source-side subfolio endpoints for each continuous Vernon, Harley 2277, and Cotton Julius D IX passage supplied by Horstmann?

next_steps: Reconstruct every abbreviation and named witness from the complete apparatus.
Do not treat the currently modeled named witnesses as exhaustive.
openQuestions.question: Which witnesses stand behind all 596 abbreviated 'al.' notes across forty-five XML divisions, and what is Horstmann's complete collation corpus?

next_steps: Ask U-M for digitization provenance or inspect METS/source metadata if made public.
Continue treating the Internet Archive PDF as a representative complete scan, not as the demonstrated parent of the U-M encoding.
openQuestions.question: Which exact physical or digital copy of Horstmann 1887 supplied U-M's page images and OCR?

next_steps: Review component-level source studies and the full witness tradition without projecting one origin or author across the anthology.
openQuestions.question: Can individual exemplars, poets, compilers, revision layers, and dates be assigned more securely across all sixty-three components?

next_steps: Check the printed incipits/explicits, manuscript sequence, and current repertory/catalog editorial files.
Contact the relevant catalog or DIMEV editors if internal evidence cannot resolve the inherited labels.
openQuestions.question: How should three source discrepancies be resolved: prologue twenty-two versus DIMEV twenty-six lines, seven versus eight lost opening items, and Edward the Martyr versus catalog/DIMEV 'Edward the Elder'?

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is the exact Horstmann/U-M edited selection, not a single homogeneous medieval work, the entire South English Legendary tradition, or the whole Laud codex.
The main and appendix target loci are discontiguous; f. 198r and f. 233v are shared boundary surfaces and are not evidence that intervening or following codex material belongs to the target.

Horstmann's Laud-base relation coexists with explicit transcribes relations for lacuna supplies from Vernon, Harley, and Cotton. Calling the edition sole-witness or diplomatic would erase material source layers.
The sixty-three component count is intellectual and repertorial, not a raw XML division count. The XML itself remains unchanged.
Complete IIIF means every canvas published by the provider at the selected local profile, not a preservation master. Caching records access and fixity, not permission to redistribute.

Date, dialect, codex production, and tradition-level compilation claims remain separated. The proposed Worcester origin is not projected onto Laud's possible Oxford production or its West Oxfordshire scribal language.

lineage.summary: The checked-in aha2708 XML is a copy of University of Michigan item AHA2708.0001.001, a 2006 uncorrected OCR-derived Level 1 encoding of Carl Horstmann's 1887 EETS OS 87 edition. The encoded medieval subject is a sixty-three-component selection from the composite Laud Misc. 108: sixty-one components in the main hand-A legendary at ff. 23r–198r and two later hand-D lives at ff. 228v–233v. Horstmann's text is Laud-based but not sole-witness: Vernon supplies most of Dunstan 107–160 and Augustine 1–36, Harley 2277 supplies two Dunstan lines plus long Nicholas and Edmund of Abingdon lacunae, and Cotton Julius D IX supplies Eustace 86–179; named and abbreviated variant readings document wider collation. The XML's sixty DIV2 containers resolve to sixty-three intellectual components through repertory boundaries and one two-division merge. A complete 564-page scan of Horstmann, the complete 496-canvas Digital Bodleian Laud facsimile, and the complete 710-canvas Vernon facsimile are cached with exact work portions. Every materially used public source file—including catalog sidecars, DIMEV data, Bell/Couch scholarship, and Taylor's dissertation—is cached, fixity-recorded, and modeled separately from the target text and from its rights layer.

catalogSummary.author: Multiple anonymous Middle English poets
catalogSummary.date: Layered medieval compilation, often placed c. 1270–1300; Laud main layer late thirteenth or early fourteenth century; appendix mid- or third-quarter fourteenth century; selection published 1887
catalogSummary.region: England; West Oxfordshire scribal dialect in the main Laud layer; proposed Worcester compilation and possible Oxford codex origin remain tentative
catalogSummary.title: The Early South-English Legendary: Laud Misc. 108 Selection

catalogingSubject.label: The Early South-English Legendary: Laud Misc. 108 Selection
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloging subject is the edition-defined selection represented by University of Michigan item AHA2708.0001.001: sixty-three intellectual Middle English verse components edited by Carl Horstmann from the South English Legendary material in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108, principally ff. 23r–198r, with the later-hand lives of Saints Blaise and Cecilia at ff. 228v–233v. It is not one homogeneous medieval work, the complete South English Legendary tradition, the whole Laud codex, a sole-witness diplomatic transcription, Horstmann's complete editorial carrier, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML.

contentParts.label: Laud Misc. 108 legendary selection

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[1–2]; 60 DIV2 containers resolved into 63 intellectual components

contentParts.label: Main legendary sequence

contentParts.label: Later-hand appendix: Saints Blaise and Cecilia
contentParts.summary: Two saint's lives from a distinct mid- or third-quarter-fourteenth-century hand-D layer, printed by Horstmann as an appendix after the main legendary.

locators.value: FRONT title page and contents; omitted preliminary PB vii–xlix plus bare PB l; BODY appendix heading
title and contents; preliminaries vii–l; appendix heading p. 484

notes: The XML carries 544 PB elements: title 2, contents 2, omitted preliminary vii–xlix 43, bare l 1, and printed pp. 1–496. It does not encode the text on preliminary pages vii–l.
contentParts.summary: Horstmann's title page and contents plus page-break scaffolding for unencoded preliminary matter and the appendix heading; this paratext is not counted among the sixty-three medieval verse components.

dateStatements.display: Layered compilation commonly placed in the later thirteenth century, often c. 1270–1285; a broader c. 1270–1300 range is safer
notes: The chronology is tradition-level reconstruction, not the date of every component or of the Laud codex.

dateStatements.display: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century

scope.description: Principal hand-A Laud selection

dateStatements.display: Middle or third quarter of the fourteenth century

scope.description: Hand-D appendix lives
First publication of this exact Horstmann edition-defined selection

extent.description: Sixty-three intellectual verse components represented by sixty XML DIV2 containers: sixty-one components in the main Laud sequence and two in the later appendix. The repository XML has 146 LG and 16,877 nonempty BODY L elements. Its 544 PB elements map title and contents pages, omitted preliminary pages vii–l, and printed pp. 1–496; front-matter text on vii–l is not encoded.

formStatements.description: The XML contains 16,877 nonempty BODY L elements in 146 LG groups and no BODY prose paragraphs.

scope.description: All sixty-three intellectual medieval components; the prose-layout manuscript presentation of Saint Lucy does not change its verse form
Modern editorial title, contents, headings, and omission scaffold outside the counted medieval components
The sixty-three medieval verse components
Medieval rubrics and occasional embedded formulae
Selected medieval rubrics in the hand-A block

notes: The selection boundary is editorial and composite: 'Part I' on the title page does not prove a missing narrative continuation, and the EETS notices project a broader standard collection, additions, glossary, and source study rather than a second half of this exact Laud text.
Manuscript date, scribal dialect, tentative codex production place, and reconstructed tradition-level compilation origin remain separate descriptive layers.
The source-cache files are research evidence and fixity records; their presence does not grant redistribution rights.

next_steps: Review component-level source studies and the full manuscript tradition without projecting a single-author model across the selection.
openQuestions.question: Can individual poets, compilers, exemplars, and revision layers be distinguished more securely across all sixty-three components?

next_steps: Collate Horstmann's line transitions against full source images or authoritative transcriptions; preserve the established broad folio loci until then.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact source-side subfolio endpoints for each continuous Vernon, Harley 2277, and Cotton Julius D IX passage supplied by Horstmann?

next_steps: Reconstruct Horstmann's complete sigla and apparatus corpus from the edition and manuscript comparisons.
openQuestions.question: Which manuscripts stand behind every abbreviated 'al.' reading in Horstmann's apparatus?

next_steps: Check DIMEV's line-count method against the scan and record editor feedback if available.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV record 392 report twenty-six lines when the XML and printed incipit/explicit boundary yield twenty-two prologue lines?

next_steps: Reconcile the catalog contents footnote's mathematically implied seven missing numbers with the headnote's statement that eight pieces are lost.
openQuestions.question: Does the Laud opening lack seven or eight legendary pieces?

next_steps: Consult catalog editors or item-specific scholarship; retain Edward the Martyr as the content label while recording the conflict.
openQuestions.question: Why do the current Bodleian catalog and DIMEV description label item 17 Edward the Elder when its incipit and narrative identify Edward the Martyr?

summaries.value: An edition-defined sixty-three-component selection from the Laud Misc. 108 recension of the South English Legendary. Its mostly calendrical sequence combines lives of saints, martyr legends, feast narratives, miracle and afterlife material, and a prologue in rhymed Middle English verse. The principal block is copied by hand A at ff. 23r–198r; Saints Blaise and Cecilia are a later hand-D appendix at ff. 228v–233v.

The XML has sixty DIV2 containers but represents sixty-three intellectual components: it splits Becket Life/Translation, the prologue/Fabian-Sebastian, both Julian lives, and the three Saint Michael parts at repertory boundaries, while merging the two XML divisions for Saints Philip and James. The count is therefore not a raw division count.

Horstmann uses Laud Misc. 108 as the principal running-text witness but explicitly supplies continuous lacunae from the Vernon Manuscript, Harley MS 2277, and Cotton MS Julius D IX, and reports a broad variant collation. The selection must not be described as a sole-witness diplomatic edition.

titles.supplied_by: Cataloger, distinguishing the encoded edition-defined selection from the wider legendary tradition

work.summary: An edition-defined sixty-three-component selection from the Laud Misc. 108 recension of the South English Legendary. Its mostly calendrical sequence combines lives of saints, martyr legends, feast narratives, miracle and afterlife material, and a prologue in rhymed Middle English verse. The principal block is copied by hand A at ff. 23r–198r; Saints Blaise and Cecilia are a later hand-D appendix at ff. 228v–233v.
